title: Selecting Evaluation Metrics
concept: Use perplexity to check general fluency, ROUGE for summarization, and human evaluation for overall style and safety. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Model Debugging.'

exercises.prompt: Why can't we use a single test to evaluate all AI text score to evaluate all aspects of an AI model with just one single test?

options.text: One single score tells us everything.
We must use different metrics for different tasks.
Only humans should ever test models.

exercises.explain: Evaluating AI is tricky because a model can write grammatically correct sentences that are completely factually incorrect or unsafe. We need multiple metrics to see the whole picture.

cards.title: How Do We Grade AI?
cards.body: How do you know if your text-writing AI is actually smart, or if it's just babbling pretty nonsense? Just like school has spelling tests, reading quizzes, and behavior grades, AI has different metrics to measure its performance.

cards.title: The Evaluation Toolkit
cards.body: We evaluate AI models using different tools for different tasks. Perplexity measures how 'surprised' a model is by normal text, proving how fluent it is. ROUGE compares generated summaries to real human summaries. Human evaluation checks for subjective things like safety, humor, and polite tone.

cards.title: The AI Chef's Kitchen
cards.body: Imagine your AI is a master chef. Checking its spelling and basic grammar (perplexity) is like ensuring they use fresh ingredients. Evaluating a recipe summary (ROUGE) is like tasting the soup to see if it matches the original flavor. But asking a food critic to eat the whole meal (human evaluation) is the only way to judge the true presentation and customer safety.

cards.title: Putting It to the Test
cards.body: Suppose you build a book summarizing bot. You first run Perplexity, and it scores well, meaning the sentences flow nicely. Next, you run ROUGE, and it shows a high overlap with your reference summaries. Finally, you hire human reviewers to read the outputs to ensure the bot doesn't recommend any bad habits to young readers.

cards.title: The Single Score Fallacy
cards.body: Many believe there is one single score that can tell you if an AI is 'good.' In reality, a model can have perfect grammar (low perplexity) but tell terrible lies or write bad summaries. You always need a mix of different tests!

cards.title: Auto vs. Human Evaluation
cards.body: Different metrics look at different aspects of text quality. Here is how automatic tools compare to human eyes.

right.text: Manual reviews that check for safety, humor, bias, and overall tone quality.

left.text: Automatic metrics that check word overlapping and general grammar rules quickly.

exercises.prompt: Which metric is best suited to quickly check if your AI model has learned basic grammar and reads fluently?
exercises.explain: Perplexity measures how predictable and fluent a sequence of words is. A lower perplexity means the model is less confused by language rules.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Perplexity is the best metric to measure how accurately an AI has summarized a 10-page document.
exercises.explain: Perplexity evaluates overall language flow, but it cannot verify if the information in a summary is accurate or matches the source. ROUGE is designed for that comparison.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct evaluation metric name:
exercises.sentence: When you want to check if your AI model successfully summarized a long article, you should use the _____ metric.
exercises.explain: ROUGE compares the words in a machine-made summary against human-written gold standards.

exercises.prompt: An AI model is generating bedtime stories, but you need to make sure the tone is warm and friendly. Which evaluation method is best?
exercises.explain: AI models can struggle with tone and safety, which are very subjective. Human judges are best at checking these qualities.

exercises.prompt: If you are building an AI chatbot to write summaries of news articles for kids, how should you divide your evaluation metrics?

options.text: Use perplexity for translation accuracy and ROUGE for safety tests.
Use ROUGE to check if text is safe and polite for kids.
Use perplexity for basic language fluency, ROUGE for summaries, and humans for safety.
Human evaluation should only be used to count the number of words.

exercises.explain: Evaluating an AI model requires matching the test to the task. Use perplexity for general language flows, ROUGE for summaries, and human eyes for nuance. Next up: Model Debugging.
